_meta.authoringNotes: Clinical and epidemiology data from CDC Zika resource (2024) and WHO Zika Fact Sheet (2024). Congenital Zika syndrome per Moore et al. JAMA Pediatr 2017 (PMID 27812690). Guillain-Barre association per Cao-Lormeau et al. Lancet 2016 (PMID 26948433). Microcephaly causal review per Rasmussen et al. NEJM 2016 (PMID 27074377).

h1Title: Zika Virus: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Zika virus is a flavivirus transmitted by Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus mosquitoes that caused a massive outbreak across the Americas in 2015-2016. WHO declared a Public Health Emergency of International Concern when the virus was linked to a sharp rise in microcephaly and other congenital brain malformations in infants born to women infected during pregnancy. Around 80% of Zika infections in adults are asymptomatic; symptomatic cases produce a mild illness with low-grade fever, maculopapular rash, conjunctivitis, and joint pain lasting 2-7 days. The major dangers are congenital Zika syndrome (severe brain damage in the fetus) and Guillain-Barre syndrome (post-infectious paralysis) in adults. No vaccine or specific antiviral therapy exists; prevention through mosquito bite avoidance, safer sex during outbreaks, and travel guidance remains the only protection.

keyStats.prevalence: More than 86 countries have reported mosquito-transmitted Zika; an estimated 1.5 million people infected in Brazil alone in 2015-2016
keyStats.demographics: Adults and children affected equally; pregnant women face the highest consequences because of vertical transmission risk
keyStats.avgAge: Symptomatic cases reported across all ages; serosurveys suggest most infections in endemic regions occur in childhood and adolescence
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 3,700 cases of congenital Zika syndrome documented during the 2015-2017 outbreak in the Americas; transmission continues at lower levels in tropical and subtropical regions

definition: Zika virus disease (ICD-10: A92.5) is an arboviral infection caused by the Zika virus, a single-stranded RNA flavivirus closely related to dengue, West Nile, and Japanese encephalitis viruses. The virus is primarily transmitted by the bite of an infected Aedes mosquito, with secondary transmission through sexual contact, blood transfusion, organ transplantation, and from mother to fetus during pregnancy or around the time of delivery. Most adult infections are asymptomatic (around 80%) or produce a mild self-limited febrile illness with rash. The clinical significance derives from two severe manifestations: congenital Zika syndrome — a pattern of microcephaly, brain calcifications, ocular abnormalities, and limb contractures in infants exposed in utero, especially during the first and early second trimesters — and Guillain-Barre syndrome, an immune-mediated polyneuropathy occurring days to weeks after infection. Outbreaks have followed introduction of the virus into populations without prior immunity; the 2015-2016 American outbreak originated in Brazil and spread through more than 60 countries within 18 months. Diagnosis combines RT-PCR for viral RNA (most sensitive in the first 7-10 days of symptoms or up to 14 days in pregnant women) with serology (IgM, plaque-reduction neutralization). Management is supportive; no licensed antiviral or vaccine exists despite multiple candidates in development.

typesClassification.description: Most common form — accounts for approximately 80% of infections in adults. No clinical illness; only detected by serosurveillance or screening during pregnancy. Still capable of vertical transmission to the fetus and sexual transmission to partners.

Mild febrile illness with low-grade fever (under 38.5°C), maculopapular rash starting on the face and spreading distally, non-purulent conjunctivitis, and joint pain (typically small joints of the hands and feet). Lasts 2-7 days and resolves without complications in most adults.

A pattern of fetal abnormalities arising from maternal infection during pregnancy: severe microcephaly with overlapping cranial sutures, subcortical calcifications, ventriculomegaly, ocular abnormalities (chorioretinal atrophy, optic nerve hypoplasia), congenital contractures (arthrogryposis), and hypertonia. First-trimester infection carries the highest risk.

Ascending symmetric paralysis developing days to weeks after acute Zika infection. Pathophysiology involves immune-mediated demyelination. Most cases recover with intravenous immunoglobulin or plasmapheresis but recovery may be prolonged.

typesClassification.type: Zika in immunocompromised hosts and adults with neurological complications
typesClassification.description: Rare reports of meningoencephalitis, myelitis, and acute disseminated encephalomyelitis in adults, especially with comorbidities. Outcomes generally favorable with supportive care.

primarySymptoms: Low-grade fever (37.5-38.5°C) developing 3-14 days after a mosquito bite in an endemic area.
Pruritic maculopapular rash starting on the face or trunk and spreading distally over 2-3 days.
Non-purulent bilateral conjunctivitis with red eyes and mild discomfort but no discharge.
Arthralgia or polyarthritis, typically of the small joints of the hands and feet, that lasts up to 1 week.
Retro-orbital pain that worsens with eye movement, often a clinical clue toward an arboviral illness.
Headache, often moderate, frontal or retro-orbital, lasting 3-5 days.
Generalized myalgia, fatigue, and malaise lasting up to 1 week.
Mild gastrointestinal symptoms — nausea, abdominal discomfort, occasionally vomiting or loose stools.
Edema of the hands or feet in a minority of patients, distinguishing Zika rash from other arbovirus rashes.
Lymphadenopathy, especially in the posterior cervical chain.
earlyWarningSigns: Maculopapular rash with low-grade fever 3-14 days after travel to or residence in an Aedes mosquito-endemic region
Non-purulent red eyes and joint pain coinciding with a febrile illness during a known Zika outbreak
Unexplained polyarthralgia of the small joints of the hands and feet in a sexually active partner of someone who recently returned from an endemic area
Concerning fetal ultrasound findings (microcephaly, intracranial calcifications, ventriculomegaly) in a pregnancy with potential Zika exposure
Ascending limb weakness or paresthesia within 1-3 weeks of a Zika-compatible illness — possible Guillain-Barre
emergencySigns: Progressive ascending weakness with areflexia, gait instability, or breathing difficulty — emergency evaluation for Guillain-Barre syndrome
Altered mental status, neck stiffness, seizures, or focal neurological deficit — exclude meningoencephalitis
Severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, or hypotension — concurrent dengue must be excluded urgently
Pregnant patient with confirmed or suspected Zika and rapidly changing fetal ultrasound — urgent referral to maternal-fetal medicine
Newborn with microcephaly, contractures, or visible brain malformations — admit for full evaluation and supportive care

causes.cause: Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus mosquito bites
causes.description: Day-biting Aedes mosquitoes acquire Zika virus by feeding on viremic humans and transmit it to others 7-10 days later. Aedes aegypti is the principal vector in tropical areas; Aedes albopictus extends transmission into temperate zones. Mosquitoes breed in small containers of standing water (flowerpots, tires, water storage drums).

Zika RNA persists in semen for up to 3 months after infection (and rarely longer) and in vaginal secretions for shorter periods. Vaginal, anal, and oral sex can transmit the virus, including from asymptomatic partners. Sexual transmission is well-documented in non-endemic countries where local mosquito spread has not occurred.

Zika crosses the placenta and directly infects fetal neural progenitor cells, interfering with neurogenesis and producing congenital Zika syndrome. Risk is highest with first-trimester maternal infection (5-15% of infants with severe defects in some cohorts) and persists through pregnancy at lower frequency.

Asymptomatic viremic donors have transmitted Zika through transfusion and transplantation. Blood services in endemic regions screen donors with PCR or by deferral of recent travelers; risk in non-endemic regions remains low but not zero.

Rare cases reported among laboratory workers handling live Zika virus and healthcare workers exposed to body fluids of viremic patients. Standard biosafety precautions effectively prevent these exposures.

riskFactors.factor: Residence or travel in a Zika-endemic region
riskFactors.description: Tropical and subtropical regions of the Americas, Caribbean, Southeast Asia, parts of Africa, and Pacific islands have ongoing or periodic Zika transmission. CDC maintains a country-by-country travel advisory updated regularly.

Pregnant women face the most serious outcomes because of fetal risk. First-trimester infection carries 5-15% risk of severe congenital abnormalities; risk persists through pregnancy. WHO and CDC advise pregnant women to avoid travel to areas of active transmission.

riskFactors.factor: Unprotected sex with a partner who has visited or lives in an endemic region
riskFactors.description: Asymptomatic male partners can transmit virus in semen for up to 3 months. CDC advises condom use or abstinence for 3 months after male and 2 months after female travel to endemic areas.
Aedes mosquitoes bite primarily during daylight hours, with peak activity around dawn and dusk. Outdoor work, sports, and travel during these hours increase exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Inadequate vector control around the home
riskFactors.description: Standing water in flowerpots, used tires, blocked gutters, water storage containers, and discarded bottles all support Aedes breeding. Eliminating these sources cuts local mosquito density dramatically.

Prior exposure to dengue may alter immune responses to Zika and complicates serological diagnosis through antibody cross-reactivity. Clinical impact on Zika severity remains debated but careful interpretation of serology is required.

affectedDemographics: Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela, and Mexico accounted for the majority of cases during the 2015-2016 American outbreak.
Pacific islands (French Polynesia, Yap, Cook Islands, Easter Island) experienced earlier outbreaks between 2007 and 2014.
Travel-associated cases reported in over 60 non-endemic countries during 2015-2017; local mosquito transmission documented in Florida, Texas, and small parts of southern Europe.
Approximately 3,700 cases of congenital Zika syndrome confirmed across the Americas 2015-2017.
Estimated 80% of infections are asymptomatic, so reported case counts substantially underestimate true transmission.
diagnosisOverview: Suspect Zika in any person with compatible symptoms (fever, maculopapular rash, conjunctivitis, arthralgia) and possible exposure (travel to or residence in an active transmission area, sexual contact with an exposed partner, or living in a region with local Aedes vectors). Pregnant women with even a suspicious exposure history warrant testing regardless of symptoms. Diagnosis uses molecular and serologic tests with timing-dependent sensitivity. Reverse-transcription PCR (RT-PCR) on serum within 7-10 days of symptom onset and on urine within 14 days is the most specific test; RT-PCR on amniotic fluid and on cord blood/neonatal serum at birth contributes to congenital diagnosis. Serology (IgM ELISA) becomes positive 4-7 days after symptom onset but cross-reacts extensively with dengue, yellow fever, West Nile, and Japanese encephalitis antibodies; positive IgM is followed by plaque-reduction neutralization testing (PRNT) for species specificity. Concurrent dengue and chikungunya testing is mandatory because the three viruses co-circulate, present similarly, and have very different management implications. In pregnancy, serial fetal ultrasound at 3-4 week intervals from the time of suspected exposure tracks head circumference, ventricular size, and brain echogenicity. Neonates exposed in utero undergo head ultrasound or MRI, ophthalmologic examination, and auditory screening at birth and again at 1, 6, and 12 months. Adult patients presenting with neurological symptoms after a compatible illness need lumbar puncture, nerve conduction studies, and infectious disease consultation for Guillain-Barre evaluation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects viral RNA during acute viremia; most specific test within 7-14 days of symptom onset
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw and a clean-catch urine sample. Results typically return in 3-7 days from reference laboratories. Serum RT-PCR is most sensitive in the first week of symptoms; urine RT-PCR extends the diagnostic window to 14 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Zika IgM ELISA serology with PRNT confirmation
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects antibody response 4 days to 12 weeks after infection; PRNT distinguishes Zika from other flaviviruses
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw. IgM result usually within 3-5 days; PRNT confirmation takes 1-3 weeks in reference labs. Positive IgM with cross-reactivity to dengue requires PRNT to interpret.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes the two main differential diagnoses, which co-circulate with Zika and have overlapping clinical features
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Done from the same blood draw used for Zika testing. Dengue NS1 antigen and IgM, plus chikungunya RT-PCR or IgM, are standard. Results in 1-7 days depending on platform.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects microcephaly, intracranial calcifications, ventriculomegaly, and other congenital Zika syndrome features during pregnancy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Transabdominal ultrasound by a maternal-fetal medicine specialist, repeated every 3-4 weeks after suspected exposure. Detailed neurosonography from the second trimester is recommended in any confirmed maternal infection.

diagnosticTests.test: Neonatal head imaging (ultrasound or MRI) and ophthalmologic examination
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms congenital Zika syndrome in newborns of women with confirmed or possible Zika infection during pregnancy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Cranial ultrasound performed at the bedside in the first days of life; MRI for additional anatomical detail. Dilated fundoscopy by a pediatric ophthalmologist identifies chorioretinal atrophy and optic nerve hypoplasia. Auditory brainstem response testing screens for hearing loss.

diagnosticTests.test: Lumbar puncture and nerve conduction studies (suspected Guillain-Barre)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents elevated CSF protein with normal cell count (albumin-cytological dissociation) and demyelinating polyneuropathy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Lumbar puncture performed by a clinician with the patient lying on their side; result the same day. Nerve conduction studies are typically uncomfortable but not painful, taking 60-90 minutes.

treatmentOverview: No specific antiviral therapy or licensed vaccine exists for Zika virus disease. Management is supportive and tailored to the clinical setting. Adults with uncomplicated symptomatic infection are treated with paracetamol (acetaminophen) for fever and pain, oral fluids, and rest. NSAIDs and aspirin are avoided until dengue has been excluded because they can worsen hemorrhagic complications in dengue. Patients are advised to use mosquito protection for the first week of illness (when viremia is highest) to prevent onward transmission. Sexual contacts are counseled on condom use for 3 months (male partner) or 2 months (female partner) after symptom onset or last possible exposure. Pregnant women with confirmed or suspected Zika are referred to maternal-fetal medicine for serial fetal ultrasound and counseling about the spectrum of possible outcomes, including the option of termination of pregnancy where legally available and consistent with the patient's values. Infants with congenital Zika syndrome require multidisciplinary follow-up with neurology, ophthalmology, audiology, physical therapy, speech and language therapy, orthopedics, and developmental pediatrics. Guillain-Barre syndrome is treated with intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG, 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days) or plasma exchange (5 sessions over 1-2 weeks), supportive care including respiratory monitoring, and rehabilitation. Public-health measures dominate the prevention landscape: vector control (source reduction, larviciding, adulticiding during outbreaks), traveler advisories, sexual health counseling, blood donor screening or deferral, and surveillance for new clusters. Several Zika vaccines have completed early-phase trials but none had been licensed at the time of writing.

medicalTreatments.name: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) 500-1000 mg every 4-6 hours as needed (max 4 g/day)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line analgesic and antipyretic for symptomatic Zika and any febrile illness when dengue cannot be excluded. Avoids the hemorrhagic risk seen with NSAIDs in dengue.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces fever and pain in over 80% of patients with mild arbovirus illnesses

medicalTreatments.name: Oral rehydration and supportive care
medicalTreatments.description: Encourages 2-3 liters per day of fluids, rest, and observation for warning signs. Hospitalization is rarely required for Zika alone but is considered if dengue is suspected or unable to be excluded.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Most cases resolve within 2-7 days with conservative care

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days (Guillain-Barre)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard treatment for moderate to severe Guillain-Barre syndrome including post-Zika cases. Reduces severity and duration of paralysis when given within 2 weeks of symptom onset.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Faster recovery to independent walking in approximately 60-70% of treated patients in Guillain-Barre cohorts

medicalTreatments.name: Plasma exchange (5 sessions over 1-2 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative first-line treatment for moderate to severe Guillain-Barre. Equivalent efficacy to IVIG; choice depends on local availability and patient comorbidity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to IVIG in reducing disability progression and time to walking

medicalTreatments.name: Multidisciplinary care for congenital Zika syndrome
medicalTreatments.description: Pediatric neurology, ophthalmology, audiology, physiotherapy, speech and language therapy, orthopedics, and developmental pediatrics involvement starting at birth. Early intervention improves functional outcomes for the spectrum of impairments.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves developmental, communication, and motor function outcomes compared with untreated cohorts

medicalTreatments.name: Antiepileptic drugs (levetiracetam, phenobarbital)
medicalTreatments.description: Used to control seizures common in congenital Zika syndrome. Choice and dose are individualized by a pediatric neurologist.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Seizure control in 60-80% of treated infants with congenital brain malformations

surgicalOptions.name: Shunting for hydrocephalus in congenital Zika syndrome
surgicalOptions.description: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement to relieve progressive hydrocephalus in selected infants with congenital Zika syndrome. Performed by pediatric neurosurgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective ventricular decompression in over 90%; shunt revision required in 30-40% within 5 years

surgicalOptions.name: Orthopedic correction of contractures (arthrogryposis)
surgicalOptions.description: Selective tendon-lengthening or joint-release procedures for severe contractures impairing positioning and care. Performed after the first year of life.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Functional improvement variable; goals are typically positional rather than ambulatory

alternativeTreatments.name: Structured early-intervention developmental programs
alternativeTreatments.description: Coordinated home-based and clinic-based services for infants with congenital Zika syndrome. Evidence from developmental disability cohorts supports earlier and more intensive programs for better motor, language, and cognitive outcomes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Family education and psychosocial support
alternativeTreatments.description: Coordinated counseling, peer support groups, and respite services reduce caregiver burnout in families of affected infants and improve adherence to long-term care plans.

whySeeSpecialist: Infectious disease and maternal-fetal medicine referral is essential for any pregnant woman with confirmed or suspected Zika exposure, and for any adult with neurological symptoms after a compatible illness. Pediatric neurology, ophthalmology, audiology, and developmental pediatrics manage newborns with congenital Zika syndrome.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an infectious disease specialist familiar with arboviral disease in your region; many academic centers run dedicated travel or tropical medicine clinics. Maternal-fetal medicine units with experience in congenital infection follow exposed pregnancies. Newborns with congenital Zika syndrome benefit from coordinated care at a tertiary children's hospital with a developmental pediatrics service.
hospitalCriteria: On-site access to Zika RT-PCR or contracted reference laboratory
Maternal-fetal medicine clinic with high-resolution fetal ultrasound and neurosonography
Pediatric neurology, ophthalmology, and audiology services for newborn evaluation
Intensive care with respiratory support for severe Guillain-Barre syndrome
Public health reporting and vector control coordination
keyFacilities: Tropical medicine and travel medicine clinics
Maternal-fetal medicine and high-risk obstetric units
Pediatric neurology and developmental medicine services
preventionStrategies: Use insect repellent containing DEET (20-30%), picaridin, IR3535, or oil of lemon eucalyptus on exposed skin and reapply per label instructions during the day in endemic regions.
Wear long sleeves, long trousers, and treated clothing (permethrin-impregnated) during peak Aedes activity (dawn and dusk).
Stay in accommodation with air conditioning and intact window screens; sleep under a permethrin-treated bed net if the room is not screened.
Eliminate breeding sites around the home: empty flowerpots, change water in bird baths twice weekly, cover or discard tires and containers, clear blocked gutters.
Use condoms or abstain from sex for at least 3 months (male partner) and 2 months (female partner) after travel to an active transmission area, even if asymptomatic.
Pregnant women and women planning pregnancy should avoid travel to areas with active Zika transmission per CDC and WHO guidance; if travel is unavoidable, apply intensified mosquito precautions.
lifestyleModifications: Postpone non-essential travel to areas with active Zika transmission, especially during pregnancy.
Use mosquito repellent diligently during the first week of illness to prevent onward transmission to others.
Counsel partners on the timeline for safer-sex precautions after possible exposure.
Stay current with travel advisories from CDC, WHO, or your country's public health agency before each trip.
Document any febrile illness with rash that occurred during or shortly after travel — this may matter for future pregnancy counseling.
Donate blood only after the deferral period (28 days or longer per local rules) following travel to an endemic area.

recommended: Adequate oral fluids (2-3 liters of water, oral rehydration solution, broths) during the acute illness
Easily digested foods (rice, broth, fruits, plain yogurt) during the febrile phase
Folate-rich foods (leafy greens, legumes, fortified grains) in women planning pregnancy
Iron-rich foods if anemia accompanies prolonged convalescence
avoid: Alcohol during the acute illness — interacts with hepatic clearance and dehydration
Aspirin and NSAIDs until dengue is excluded — they can worsen hemorrhagic complications in dengue
Unsafe street food or untreated water that may add an enteric infection on top of viral illness
Unproven herbal 'remedies' marketed for Zika — none have evidence of antiviral activity

exerciseGuidelines: Rest during the acute febrile illness. Most adults resume normal activity within 1-2 weeks. Patients with post-Zika Guillain-Barre syndrome follow structured neurological rehabilitation with progressive mobilization, strengthening, and gait training under physiotherapy. Pregnant women with Zika exposure continue routine pregnancy exercise unless complicated by other obstetric issues.
dailyManagement: Take paracetamol (acetaminophen) for fever and aches; avoid aspirin and NSAIDs until dengue is excluded.
Drink at least 2-3 liters of fluid each day during the acute illness.
Use mosquito repellent and stay in screened or air-conditioned accommodation through the first week of symptoms.
Use condoms or abstain from sex for the recommended duration after travel or symptoms.
Attend prenatal follow-up with serial ultrasound if pregnant and exposed.
Report any new neurological symptoms (limb weakness, paresthesia, breathing difficulty) urgently.
prognosis: Outcomes vary dramatically by clinical form. Symptomatic acute Zika in non-pregnant adults has an excellent prognosis: more than 95% recover fully within 1-2 weeks without sequelae. Post-Zika Guillain-Barre syndrome has a more variable course; most patients improve over 6-12 months with treatment but a minority have residual weakness or chronic pain. Congenital Zika syndrome carries a guarded long-term prognosis: surviving infants have lifelong neurodevelopmental disability with severe motor impairment, epilepsy, sensory deficits, and feeding difficulties. Mortality in the first year of life is around 5-10% in case series, and ongoing care needs are substantial. The 2015-2016 outbreak has been followed by lower-level endemic transmission in many countries; recurrent outbreaks are anticipated whenever vector density rises and population immunity wanes. Vaccine development is active and a licensed product would change this prognosis dramatically.
recoveryTimeline: Acute symptoms in adults resolve over 2-7 days with full recovery in 1-2 weeks. Joint pain may linger for weeks in some patients. Guillain-Barre recovery is over months; most patients walk independently again by 6-12 months. Congenital Zika syndrome is lifelong; developmental gains continue with sustained therapy but full functional recovery does not occur.
complications: Congenital Zika syndrome in infants of exposed mothers — microcephaly, brain calcifications, contractures, ocular and hearing impairment; lifelong disability requiring multidisciplinary care.
Guillain-Barre syndrome in adults — ascending paralysis with potential need for ventilatory support; mortality 3-7% in untreated cases, lower with prompt IVIG or plasma exchange.
Meningoencephalitis or myelitis in rare adult cases — present with altered consciousness, seizures, or focal neurological deficits.
Pregnancy loss (miscarriage, stillbirth) in a subset of confirmed maternal infections.
Sexual onward transmission to partners, sometimes weeks to months after the index case has recovered.
Co-infection with dengue or chikungunya carrying additional risks of hemorrhagic disease or chronic arthralgia.

supportResources.name: CDC Zika Virus
supportResources.description: Current US public health guidance on diagnosis, prevention, traveler advisories, and pregnancy outcomes.

supportResources.name: WHO Zika Virus Disease
supportResources.description: Global epidemiology, prevention guidance, and emergency response framework for Zika outbreaks.

supportResources.name: March of Dimes — Zika and pregnancy
supportResources.description: Patient-facing pregnancy guidance, support resources, and education on congenital infections.

supportResources.name: Pan American Health Organization (PAHO)
supportResources.description: Regional public health information for the Americas, where most major Zika outbreaks have occurred.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dengue Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dengue and Zika are transmitted by the same Aedes mosquito and share fever, rash, headache, and arthralgia. Dengue causes higher fever, greater bone pain, thrombocytopenia, and potential hemorrhagic complications; Zika has lower fever, prominent conjunctivitis, and the pregnancy and Guillain-Barre risks. Both must be tested in any compatible patient.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chikungunya
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chikungunya causes severe disabling polyarthralgia that can persist for months, with fever and rash sharing features with Zika. Joint pain is far more prominent in chikungunya. Both are transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes and frequently co-circulate; serology and PCR distinguish them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Yellow Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Yellow fever causes high fever, jaundice, hemorrhage, and high mortality. It is prevented by a single-dose live vaccine that is required for travel to many endemic countries. Zika is comparatively mild in adults but carries the pregnancy and neurological risks not seen in yellow fever.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rubella
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rubella also causes a maculopapular rash, lymphadenopathy, and fetal congenital syndrome in non-immune pregnant women. Distinguished by vaccine history (MMR), exposure history, and specific serology. Both can cause congenital syndromes but via different mechanisms.

relatedConditions.name: Dengue Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Same Aedes mosquito vector, overlapping clinical features, and concurrent testing essential. Co-infections are common in endemic regions.

relatedConditions.name: Chikungunya
relatedConditions.relevance: Shared vector and overlapping clinical features; differential diagnosis and concurrent testing recommended.

relatedConditions.name: Yellow Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Another flavivirus disease transmitted by mosquitoes; vaccination is available and required for travel to many endemic countries.

relatedConditions.name: Guillain-Barre Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Major post-infectious complication of Zika in adults; one of the principal reasons for hospitalization during outbreaks.

relatedConditions.name: Microcephaly
relatedConditions.relevance: Hallmark feature of congenital Zika syndrome; Zika is now recognized as a major infectious cause of microcephaly in endemic outbreaks.

faqs.question: What is Zika virus?
faqs.answer: Zika is a flavivirus transmitted by Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus mosquitoes that causes a mild illness with fever, rash, conjunctivitis, and joint pain in adults. Its major risks are birth defects in babies born to women infected during pregnancy and Guillain-Barre syndrome in adults. No vaccine or specific antiviral is available.

faqs.question: How is Zika virus transmitted?
faqs.answer: Primarily through the bite of infected Aedes mosquitoes that bite during the day. Zika also spreads through unprotected sex with an infected partner (virus persists in semen for up to 3 months), from mother to fetus during pregnancy, and rarely through blood transfusion or organ transplantation.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of Zika?
faqs.answer: About 80% of infections cause no symptoms. Symptomatic illness produces low-grade fever, itchy maculopapular rash, non-purulent conjunctivitis (red eyes), and joint pain in the hands and feet, lasting 2-7 days. Headache, retro-orbital pain, muscle aches, and fatigue are common. Severe complications are rare in adults.

faqs.question: Is Zika dangerous during pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Zika crosses the placenta and can cause congenital Zika syndrome — microcephaly, brain calcifications, ventriculomegaly, ocular abnormalities, and contractures. First-trimester infection carries the highest risk; an estimated 5-15% of infants exposed in the first trimester develop severe abnormalities.

faqs.question: How is Zika diagnosed?
faqs.answer: RT-PCR on serum within 7-10 days or urine within 14 days of symptom onset detects viral RNA. After that window, IgM serology is used, followed by plaque-reduction neutralization testing to distinguish Zika from dengue and other flaviviruses. Pregnant women with possible exposure should be tested regardless of symptoms.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for Zika?
faqs.answer: No licensed Zika vaccine is currently available. Several candidates have completed early-phase trials and continue in development. Prevention relies on mosquito bite avoidance, safer sex practices after potential exposure, and travel advisories for pregnant women.

faqs.question: How long does Zika stay in semen?
faqs.answer: Zika RNA has been detected in semen for up to 3 months after infection, with rare cases extending further. CDC and WHO recommend condom use or abstinence for at least 3 months after travel to an active transmission area for male partners, and 2 months for female partners, even if asymptomatic.

faqs.question: Can men transmit Zika sexually if they have no symptoms?
faqs.answer: Yes. Asymptomatic men have transmitted Zika to female partners through unprotected sex. Because up to 80% of infections are asymptomatic, sexual prevention guidance applies after any travel to an active transmission area regardless of whether symptoms occurred.

faqs.question: How is Zika treated?
faqs.answer: There is no specific antiviral therapy. Treatment is supportive — paracetamol for fever and pain, oral fluids, rest, and mosquito protection during the first week of illness to prevent onward transmission. Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs until dengue has been excluded.

faqs.question: Where is Zika virus found?
faqs.answer: Tropical and subtropical regions of the Americas, Caribbean, Southeast Asia, parts of Africa, and Pacific islands have ongoing or periodic Zika transmission. CDC and WHO maintain travel advisories with country-by-country guidance updated regularly.

faqs.question: Can Zika be transmitted through breastfeeding?
faqs.answer: Zika RNA has been detected in breast milk, but transmission through breastfeeding has not been clearly documented. WHO continues to recommend breastfeeding even in mothers with confirmed Zika because the proven benefits outweigh the theoretical risk.

faqs.question: What is congenital Zika syndrome?
faqs.answer: Congenital Zika syndrome is a recognizable pattern of severe brain and limb abnormalities in babies born to women infected during pregnancy. Features include microcephaly with overlapping skull sutures, subcortical brain calcifications, ventriculomegaly, ocular abnormalities, joint contractures, and hypertonia.

faqs.question: Does prior dengue protect against Zika?
faqs.answer: No. Prior dengue infection does not prevent Zika, although it may modify the immune response and complicate serological diagnosis through antibody cross-reactivity. Clinical impact on Zika severity remains debated and careful interpretation of serology is needed.

faqs.question: What is the link between Zika and Guillain-Barre?
faqs.answer: Adult Zika infection can trigger Guillain-Barre syndrome, an immune-mediated ascending paralysis, days to weeks after the acute illness. Recognized during the 2015-2016 outbreak in French Polynesia and the Americas, it is treated with intravenous immunoglobulin or plasma exchange and supportive care.

faqs.question: Should I cancel travel because of Zika?
faqs.answer: Pregnant women and women planning pregnancy should avoid travel to areas with active Zika transmission per CDC and WHO. Other travelers should weigh personal risk, follow strict mosquito-bite prevention, and observe safer-sex precautions for 2-3 months after return. Check current travel advisories before booking.

faqs.question: What is the best mosquito repellent for Zika prevention?
faqs.answer: Insect repellents containing DEET (20-30%), picaridin, IR3535, or oil of lemon eucalyptus offer effective protection against Aedes mosquitoes. Apply per label instructions, reapply during the day, and use on top of sunscreen. Combine with permethrin-treated clothing for the highest protection.

faqs.question: Can children get Zika?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children can be infected and most cases are mild, with fever, rash, and joint symptoms similar to adults. Severe pediatric complications are rare. Congenital Zika syndrome refers specifically to in-utero infection rather than infection acquired by a child after birth.

faqs.question: Is Zika still spreading in 2024 and 2025?
faqs.answer: Yes, at lower levels than during the 2015-2016 outbreak. Sporadic and seasonal transmission continues in many endemic regions; small outbreaks have been reported in Brazil, India, and parts of Southeast Asia. Recurrent outbreaks are likely when vector density rises and population immunity wanes.

faqs.question: Can Zika cause death?
faqs.answer: Death from Zika is uncommon in adults; most fatalities are linked to severe Guillain-Barre syndrome or other rare neurological complications, especially in patients with underlying conditions. Infant mortality in severe congenital Zika syndrome reaches 5-10% in the first year of life.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Zika and dengue?
faqs.answer: Both are transmitted by the same Aedes mosquito and cause fever and rash, but dengue typically produces higher fever, severe bone pain, low platelets, and potential hemorrhage. Zika causes lower fever, prominent conjunctivitis, and the unique risks of congenital infection and Guillain-Barre. Both must be tested in any compatible patient.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for possible Zika?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if you develop fever, rash, joint pain, or red eyes within two weeks of travel to an endemic area or sex with an exposed partner. Pregnant women with any possible exposure should seek care promptly. Urgent evaluation is needed for limb weakness, breathing difficulty, or fetal ultrasound concerns.

metaTitle: Zika Virus: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Zika virus: mosquito-borne illness causing fever, rash, and serious birth defects. Symptoms, pregnancy risk, diagnosis, prevention and treatment, reviewed.
